answers:
**1. Production configuration values that should not ship (all on branch `main`, commit `2f6572d`):**

In `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars`:

- **Line 2, `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR = "true"`** — enables the debug toolbar in production (`backend/config.py:8` turns this into `enable_debug_toolbar=True`). Change to `"false"`.
- **Line 3, `AUTH_RELAXED = "true"`** — relaxed authentication (`backend/config.py:9`) in a multi-tenant clinic-scheduling product is a security hole. Change to `"false"`.
- **Line 4, `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS = "qa@example.com:superadmin"`** — pre-seeds a QA account as superadmin in production. Change to `""` (empty; `backend/config.py:10` defaults to empty when unset) or remove the key.
- **Line 6, `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK = "s3://medislot-shadow/staging/observations/"`** — points production at the *staging* S3 sink. This is live even though line 5 sets `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED = "false"`, because `backend/config.py:18-22` (`record_shadow_observation`) explicitly writes whenever a sink URI is configured and "does not consult shadow_scoring_enabled" (its own comment, lines 19-20). As written, production patient-scheduling observations would be written into the staging bucket. Change to `""` (or a dedicated prod sink if shadow scoring is actually wanted).
- **Line 7, `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN = "arn:synthetic:secrets:legacy-billing-key"`** — identical to staging's value (`infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars:7`), so production would share staging's legacy billing secret. This one needs a decision rather than a mechanical fix: point it at a production billing secret, or remove it if the `0003_billing` migration path supersedes it.

In `infra/prod/main.tf`:

- **Line 3, `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"`** — the production deploy role trusts the GitHub OIDC subject for the **staging** environment. Change to `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production` so only jobs running in the `production` GitHub environment (with its approval gates) can assume the prod deploy role. As committed, staging deploy jobs could deploy to production, and production-environment jobs could not authenticate at all.
- **Related module defect:** `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:7` hardcodes `name = "medislot-deploy"`, and `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate` (lines 9-13) shows a role by exactly that name already exists from staging's apply. Applying `infra/prod` as-is would collide with (or clobber) the staging role. The module needs a name variable (e.g. `medislot-deploy-prod`), which `infra/prod/main.tf:1-4` must then set.

The `git diff 28f07ee 2f6572d` confirms the teammate changed only the service name (`medislot-prod`, `infra/prod/main.tf:7`) when copying; every setting above is a verbatim staging leftover.

**2. How the production frontend is built and deployed, and where the API base URL comes from:**

There is exactly one frontend build for both environments. On push to `main`, the `build-frontend` job in `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` runs `npm ci` then `npx vite build --mode staging` (line 14) in `frontend/`. Vite's `--mode staging` loads `frontend/.env.staging`, whose only line is `VITE_API_BASE_URL=https://staging-api.example.com`. Per `frontend/vite.config.ts:3` ("VITE_-prefixed variables are inlined into the built bundle at build time") and `frontend/src.txt` (the app reads `import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL` at build time), that URL is baked into the JavaScript bundle at build time — it is not a runtime setting. The bundle is then packaged into a container image, pushed to `registry.example.com/medislot:latest`, and its digest is captured (workflow lines 16-21). The `deploy-prod` job (lines 29-35) deploys **that same digest** to prod. Consequence: the production frontend, as configured, would ship with the staging API base URL hard-coded into it, and every production user's browser would call `https://staging-api.example.com`. There is no `frontend/.env.production` in the repo (only `.env.staging`, `src.txt`, `vite.config.ts` per `ls frontend`), so fixing this requires both adding that file with the production API URL and changing the pipeline — either a separate production build/image or a runtime-injected config, since a single image built `--mode staging` cannot serve both environments.

**3. What the deploy pipeline does for this first production release, and what's missing:**

`.github/workflows/deploy.yml`, triggered on push to `main`:

1. **`build-frontend`** — checks out the repo, `npm ci`, `npx vite build --mode staging`, then `docker build -t registry.example.com/medislot:latest .`, `docker push`, and exports the image digest as a job output.
2. **`deploy-staging`** — `needs: build-frontend`, runs in GitHub environment `staging`, and runs `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> staging`.
3. **`deploy-prod`** — `needs: [build-frontend, deploy-staging]`, runs in GitHub environment `production`, and runs `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> prod`, i.e. promotes the same already-built digest.

Missing or broken pieces the release requires:

- **`scripts/deploy.sh` does not exist.** `find . -name deploy.sh` over the whole repo returns nothing; there is no `scripts/` directory at all (`ls -la` of the repo root shows only `.git .github backend docs frontend infra README.md`). Both deploy jobs would fail at their only step.
- **No `Dockerfile` exists** (`find . -name Dockerfile` returns nothing), so `docker build … .` in `build-frontend` would also fail.
- **The deploy jobs never check out the repo.** Only `build-frontend` has `actions/checkout@v4`; `deploy-staging` and `deploy-prod` start from an empty workspace, so even a committed `./scripts/deploy.sh` would not be present when they run.
- **No migration step is visible anywhere in the pipeline**, yet `docs/prod-runbook.md` (last updated "3 months ago", predating the prod work) claims "Database migrations are applied automatically during deploy." With three migrations pending for a brand-new prod database (`backend/migrations/versions/0001_base.py`, `0002_tenants.py`, `0003_billing.py`) and no `deploy.sh` to inspect, there is no verifiable mechanism that would apply them on first launch.
- **No cloud-auth/role-assumption step in the deploy jobs** (no OIDC token exchange, no credentials action) — connected to answer 4.
- **`infra/prod` is not applied by anything.** The workflow contains no Terraform steps, and `infra/prod/README.md` confirms "Not yet applied" — someone must apply it (after fixing answer 1) before `deploy-prod` has a `medislot-prod` service to deploy to.
- Minor: the image is tagged mutable `:latest` only; deploys use the digest so promotion is safe, but concurrent pipeline runs can race on the tag.

**4. How CI authenticates to deploy staging vs production:**

The intended mechanism is GitHub OIDC federation into a cloud IAM role. `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf` creates `synthetic_iam_role.deploy` (named `medislot-deploy`, line 7) whose `assume_role_condition` matches the GitHub OIDC token's `sub` claim (`token.actions.example.com:sub`, line 9) against a per-environment value. For **staging**, `infra/staging/main.tf:3` sets that condition to `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging`, and the `deploy-staging` job runs with `environment: staging` (`deploy.yml:25`), so its OIDC token carries exactly that subject and can assume the applied role (which exists — `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate` lines 9-13). For **production**, the `deploy-prod` job runs with `environment: production` (`deploy.yml:32`), so its OIDC token's subject will be `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production` — but the not-yet-applied `infra/prod/main.tf:3` still trusts `…environment:staging`. So as committed, production CI **cannot** authenticate (its token won't match the trust condition), while staging jobs *would* be trusted by the production deploy role — the inverse of what's wanted. Additionally, both environments' modules would create a role with the identical hardcoded name `medislot-deploy`, conflicting with the existing staging role on apply. And in the workflow itself, neither deploy job currently contains any step that performs the OIDC exchange (no `id-token: write` permission, no credentials action) — presumably that lives in the missing `deploy.sh` or was never written.
report:
All the evidence is in. Writing up the report now.

## Plan

I set out to review `medislot/infra/prod/` before its first `terraform apply`, checking four things: (1) every production config value against what a real production launch requires, (2) how the frontend bundle gets its API base URL and whether the production deploy ships the right one, (3) each step of the deploy pipeline and whether anything it references actually exists, and (4) the CI-to-cloud authentication path for staging versus production. My method was to read every file in the repo (it is small), diff prod against staging, and check the committed staging Terraform state to see what already exists.

## Actions

All paths relative to `/private/var/folders/pn/…/s2p-cutover-t1-cold/medislot` unless noted. No files were modified; all commands were read-only.

1. `find`/`ls` over the work directory — found many sibling projects; identified `medislot/` as the target repo.
2. `git branch -a && git log --oneline` in `medislot/` — single branch `main`, two commits: `28f07ee` (staging env, pipeline, runbook) and `2f6572d` (REL-140, production infra copied from staging).
3. Read `infra/prod/main.tf`, `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars`, `infra/prod/README.md` — the production Terraform: deploy role with a staging OIDC subject, service `medislot-prod`, staging-flavored app settings.
4. Read `infra/staging/main.tf`, `infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars`, `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf` — confirmed prod is a near-verbatim copy of staging; the deploy-role module hardcodes the role name `medislot-deploy`.
5. Read `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` — the three-job pipeline (build-frontend → deploy-staging → deploy-prod).
6. Read `frontend/.env.staging`, `frontend/vite.config.ts`, `frontend/src.txt`, and `backend/config.py` — how `VITE_API_BASE_URL` is inlined at build time, and how the backend consumes the app settings (notably that `record_shadow_observation` ignores `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED` and writes whenever a sink URI is set).
7. Read `docs/prod-runbook.md`, `README.md`, `backend/migrations/versions/` (listed all three; read `0003_billing.py`) — the runbook's claims about automated deploys and migrations.
8. Read `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate`; `ls -la` the repo root; `find` for `deploy.sh` and `Dockerfile` — confirmed the staging role `medislot-deploy` already exists in state, and that **neither `scripts/deploy.sh` nor any `Dockerfile` exists anywhere in the repo**.
9. `git show 2f6572d --stat` and `git diff 28f07ee 2f6572d` — confirmed the prod commit only added the three `infra/prod/` files, changing nothing but the service name and README versus staging.

No external guides, playbooks, or reference documents shaped the approach; the only in-repo document consulted was `docs/prod-runbook.md`, which I treated as a claim to verify rather than an instruction.

## Answers

**1. Production configuration values that should not ship (all on branch `main`, commit `2f6572d`):**

In `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars`:

- **Line 2, `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR = "true"`** — enables the debug toolbar in production (`backend/config.py:8` turns this into `enable_debug_toolbar=True`). Change to `"false"`.
- **Line 3, `AUTH_RELAXED = "true"`** — relaxed authentication (`backend/config.py:9`) in a multi-tenant clinic-scheduling product is a security hole. Change to `"false"`.
- **Line 4, `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS = "qa@example.com:superadmin"`** — pre-seeds a QA account as superadmin in production. Change to `""` (empty; `backend/config.py:10` defaults to empty when unset) or remove the key.
- **Line 6, `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK = "s3://medislot-shadow/staging/observations/"`** — points production at the *staging* S3 sink. This is live even though line 5 sets `SHADOW_SCORING_ENABLED = "false"`, because `backend/config.py:18-22` (`record_shadow_observation`) explicitly writes whenever a sink URI is configured and "does not consult shadow_scoring_enabled" (its own comment, lines 19-20). As written, production patient-scheduling observations would be written into the staging bucket. Change to `""` (or a dedicated prod sink if shadow scoring is actually wanted).
- **Line 7, `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN = "arn:synthetic:secrets:legacy-billing-key"`** — identical to staging's value (`infra/staging/app.auto.tfvars:7`), so production would share staging's legacy billing secret. This one needs a decision rather than a mechanical fix: point it at a production billing secret, or remove it if the `0003_billing` migration path supersedes it.

In `infra/prod/main.tf`:

- **Line 3, `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging"`** — the production deploy role trusts the GitHub OIDC subject for the **staging** environment. Change to `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production` so only jobs running in the `production` GitHub environment (with its approval gates) can assume the prod deploy role. As committed, staging deploy jobs could deploy to production, and production-environment jobs could not authenticate at all.
- **Related module defect:** `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf:7` hardcodes `name = "medislot-deploy"`, and `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate` (lines 9-13) shows a role by exactly that name already exists from staging's apply. Applying `infra/prod` as-is would collide with (or clobber) the staging role. The module needs a name variable (e.g. `medislot-deploy-prod`), which `infra/prod/main.tf:1-4` must then set.

The `git diff 28f07ee 2f6572d` confirms the teammate changed only the service name (`medislot-prod`, `infra/prod/main.tf:7`) when copying; every setting above is a verbatim staging leftover.

**2. How the production frontend is built and deployed, and where the API base URL comes from:**

There is exactly one frontend build for both environments. On push to `main`, the `build-frontend` job in `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` runs `npm ci` then `npx vite build --mode staging` (line 14) in `frontend/`. Vite's `--mode staging` loads `frontend/.env.staging`, whose only line is `VITE_API_BASE_URL=https://staging-api.example.com`. Per `frontend/vite.config.ts:3` ("VITE_-prefixed variables are inlined into the built bundle at build time") and `frontend/src.txt` (the app reads `import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL` at build time), that URL is baked into the JavaScript bundle at build time — it is not a runtime setting. The bundle is then packaged into a container image, pushed to `registry.example.com/medislot:latest`, and its digest is captured (workflow lines 16-21). The `deploy-prod` job (lines 29-35) deploys **that same digest** to prod. Consequence: the production frontend, as configured, would ship with the staging API base URL hard-coded into it, and every production user's browser would call `https://staging-api.example.com`. There is no `frontend/.env.production` in the repo (only `.env.staging`, `src.txt`, `vite.config.ts` per `ls frontend`), so fixing this requires both adding that file with the production API URL and changing the pipeline — either a separate production build/image or a runtime-injected config, since a single image built `--mode staging` cannot serve both environments.

**3. What the deploy pipeline does for this first production release, and what's missing:**

`.github/workflows/deploy.yml`, triggered on push to `main`:

1. **`build-frontend`** — checks out the repo, `npm ci`, `npx vite build --mode staging`, then `docker build -t registry.example.com/medislot:latest .`, `docker push`, and exports the image digest as a job output.
2. **`deploy-staging`** — `needs: build-frontend`, runs in GitHub environment `staging`, and runs `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> staging`.
3. **`deploy-prod`** — `needs: [build-frontend, deploy-staging]`, runs in GitHub environment `production`, and runs `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> prod`, i.e. promotes the same already-built digest.

Missing or broken pieces the release requires:

- **`scripts/deploy.sh` does not exist.** `find . -name deploy.sh` over the whole repo returns nothing; there is no `scripts/` directory at all (`ls -la` of the repo root shows only `.git .github backend docs frontend infra README.md`). Both deploy jobs would fail at their only step.
- **No `Dockerfile` exists** (`find . -name Dockerfile` returns nothing), so `docker build … .` in `build-frontend` would also fail.
- **The deploy jobs never check out the repo.** Only `build-frontend` has `actions/checkout@v4`; `deploy-staging` and `deploy-prod` start from an empty workspace, so even a committed `./scripts/deploy.sh` would not be present when they run.
- **No migration step is visible anywhere in the pipeline**, yet `docs/prod-runbook.md` (last updated "3 months ago", predating the prod work) claims "Database migrations are applied automatically during deploy." With three migrations pending for a brand-new prod database (`backend/migrations/versions/0001_base.py`, `0002_tenants.py`, `0003_billing.py`) and no `deploy.sh` to inspect, there is no verifiable mechanism that would apply them on first launch.
- **No cloud-auth/role-assumption step in the deploy jobs** (no OIDC token exchange, no credentials action) — connected to answer 4.
- **`infra/prod` is not applied by anything.** The workflow contains no Terraform steps, and `infra/prod/README.md` confirms "Not yet applied" — someone must apply it (after fixing answer 1) before `deploy-prod` has a `medislot-prod` service to deploy to.
- Minor: the image is tagged mutable `:latest` only; deploys use the digest so promotion is safe, but concurrent pipeline runs can race on the tag.

**4. How CI authenticates to deploy staging vs production:**

The intended mechanism is GitHub OIDC federation into a cloud IAM role. `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf` creates `synthetic_iam_role.deploy` (named `medislot-deploy`, line 7) whose `assume_role_condition` matches the GitHub OIDC token's `sub` claim (`token.actions.example.com:sub`, line 9) against a per-environment value. For **staging**, `infra/staging/main.tf:3` sets that condition to `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging`, and the `deploy-staging` job runs with `environment: staging` (`deploy.yml:25`), so its OIDC token carries exactly that subject and can assume the applied role (which exists — `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate` lines 9-13). For **production**, the `deploy-prod` job runs with `environment: production` (`deploy.yml:32`), so its OIDC token's subject will be `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production` — but the not-yet-applied `infra/prod/main.tf:3` still trusts `…environment:staging`. So as committed, production CI **cannot** authenticate (its token won't match the trust condition), while staging jobs *would* be trusted by the production deploy role — the inverse of what's wanted. Additionally, both environments' modules would create a role with the identical hardcoded name `medislot-deploy`, conflicting with the existing staging role on apply. And in the workflow itself, neither deploy job currently contains any step that performs the OIDC exchange (no `id-token: write` permission, no credentials action) — presumably that lives in the missing `deploy.sh` or was never written.

## Next steps

If I could make changes, in order:

1. Fix `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars`: `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR="false"`, `AUTH_RELAXED="false"`, `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS=""`, `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK=""`, and get a decision from the billing owner on a production `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN`.
2. Parameterize the role name in `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf` (e.g. `medislot-deploy-${var.env}`), set `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production"` in `infra/prod/main.tf`, and re-apply staging with an explicit name to avoid drift.
3. Add `frontend/.env.production` with the real production API URL and split the pipeline into per-environment builds (or move the API base URL to runtime config), since one `--mode staging` image cannot serve both.
4. Write and commit `scripts/deploy.sh` and a `Dockerfile`; add `actions/checkout` plus an OIDC credentials step (`id-token: write`) to both deploy jobs; add an explicit, visible migration step.
5. Plan the first prod release order: apply `infra/prod`, provision/verify the prod database and run migrations 0001–0003, then run the pipeline; update `docs/prod-runbook.md`, which is three months stale and predates all of this.
6. Get all of the above reviewed and only then `terraform apply infra/prod`.

## Confidence and gaps

- **Answer 1 — high confidence.** Every flagged value is directly visible in `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` and `infra/prod/main.tf`, and `git diff 28f07ee 2f6572d` proves they are unmodified staging copies. The shadow-sink risk rests on `backend/config.py:18-22`, which I read directly. The one judgment call is `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN` — sharing a secret between staging and prod is suspect, but I can't see the secret's contents or intended scope, so "needs a decision" rather than "definitely wrong."
- **Answer 2 — high confidence** on the mechanism and the staging-URL-in-prod defect. Caveat: `frontend/src.txt` is a placeholder describing the app rather than real React source, so I'm trusting its statement (consistent with `vite.config.ts`'s comment and standard Vite behavior) that the app reads `import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL`.
- **Answer 3 — high confidence** that `scripts/deploy.sh` and a `Dockerfile` are absent (exhaustive `find` over the repo) and that the deploy jobs lack checkout. The migration gap is a documented-claim-vs-missing-mechanism finding; I cannot rule out that migrations were intended to run inside the missing `deploy.sh`.
- **Answer 4 — high confidence** on the trust-condition mismatch and the role-name collision, both grounded in the module source, both environments' `main.tf`, and the staging tfstate. Gaps: this repo uses synthetic resources (`synthetic_iam_role`, `token.actions.example.com`), so I'm reasoning from the declared semantics rather than a real cloud provider; and I cannot inspect the GitHub-side `staging`/`production` environment settings (approval rules, secrets) from the repo alone. I also could not run `terraform validate/plan` — no state mutation was permitted and no providers are initialized — so the name-collision claim is inferred from the hardcoded name plus existing state rather than an observed plan error.

On push to `main`, the `build-frontend` job in `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` runs `npm ci` then `npx vite build --mode staging` (line 14) in `frontend/`. Vite's `--mode staging` loads `frontend/.env.staging`, whose only line is `VITE_API_BASE_URL=https://staging-api.example.com`. Per `frontend/vite.config.ts:3` ("VITE_-prefixed variables are inlined into the built bundle at build time") and `frontend/src.txt` (the app reads `import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL` at build time), that URL is baked into the JavaScript bundle at build time — it is not a runtime setting. The bundle is then packaged into a container image, pushed to `registry.example.com/medislot:latest`, and its digest is captured (workflow lines 16-21). The `deploy-prod` job (lines 29-35) deploys **that same digest** to prod. Consequence: the production frontend, as configured, would ship with the staging API base URL hard-coded into it, and every production user's browser would call `https://staging-api.example.com`. There is no `frontend/.env.production` in the repo (only `.env.staging`, `src.txt`, `vite.config.ts` per `ls frontend`), so fixing this requires both adding that file with the production API URL and changing the pipeline — either a separate production build/image or a runtime-injected config, since a single image built `--mode staging` cannot serve both environments.

**3. What the deploy pipeline does for this first production release, and what's missing:**

`.github/workflows/deploy.yml`, triggered on push to `main`:

1. **`build-frontend`** — checks out the repo, `npm ci`, `npx vite build --mode staging`, then `docker build -t registry.example.com/medislot:latest .`, `docker push`, and exports the image digest as a job output.
2. **`deploy-staging`** — `needs: build-frontend`, runs in GitHub environment `staging`, and runs `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> staging`.
3. **`deploy-prod`** — `needs: [build-frontend, deploy-staging]`, runs in GitHub environment `production`, and runs `./scripts/deploy.sh <digest> prod`, i.e. promotes the same already-built digest.

Missing or broken pieces the release requires:

- **`scripts/deploy.sh` does not exist.** `find . -name deploy.sh` over the whole repo returns nothing; there is no `scripts/` directory at all (`ls -la` of the repo root shows only `.git .github backend docs frontend infra README.md`). Both deploy jobs would fail at their only step.
- **No `Dockerfile` exists** (`find . -name Dockerfile` returns nothing), so `docker build … .` in `build-frontend` would also fail.
- **The deploy jobs never check out the repo.** Only `build-frontend` has `actions/checkout@v4`; `deploy-staging` and `deploy-prod` start from an empty workspace, so even a committed `./scripts/deploy.sh` would not be present when they run.
- **No migration step is visible anywhere in the pipeline**, yet `docs/prod-runbook.md` (last updated "3 months ago", predating the prod work) claims "Database migrations are applied automatically during deploy." With three migrations pending for a brand-new prod database (`backend/migrations/versions/0001_base.py`, `0002_tenants.py`, `0003_billing.py`) and no `deploy.sh` to inspect, there is no verifiable mechanism that would apply them on first launch.
- **No cloud-auth/role-assumption step in the deploy jobs** (no OIDC token exchange, no credentials action) — connected to answer 4.
- **`infra/prod` is not applied by anything.** The workflow contains no Terraform steps, and `infra/prod/README.md` confirms "Not yet applied" — someone must apply it (after fixing answer 1) before `deploy-prod` has a `medislot-prod` service to deploy to.
- Minor: the image is tagged mutable `:latest` only; deploys use the digest so promotion is safe, but concurrent pipeline runs can race on the tag.

**4. How CI authenticates to deploy staging vs production:**

The intended mechanism is GitHub OIDC federation into a cloud IAM role. `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf` creates `synthetic_iam_role.deploy` (named `medislot-deploy`, line 7) whose `assume_role_condition` matches the GitHub OIDC token's `sub` claim (`token.actions.example.com:sub`, line 9) against a per-environment value. For **staging**, `infra/staging/main.tf:3` sets that condition to `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:staging`, and the `deploy-staging` job runs with `environment: staging` (`deploy.yml:25`), so its OIDC token carries exactly that subject and can assume the applied role (which exists — `infra/staging/terraform.tfstate` lines 9-13). For **production**, the `deploy-prod` job runs with `environment: production` (`deploy.yml:32`), so its OIDC token's subject will be `repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production` — but the not-yet-applied `infra/prod/main.tf:3` still trusts `…environment:staging`. So as committed, production CI **cannot** authenticate (its token won't match the trust condition), while staging jobs *would* be trusted by the production deploy role — the inverse of what's wanted. Additionally, both environments' modules would create a role with the identical hardcoded name `medislot-deploy`, conflicting with the existing staging role on apply. And in the workflow itself, neither deploy job currently contains any step that performs the OIDC exchange (no `id-token: write` permission, no credentials action) — presumably that lives in the missing `deploy.sh` or was never written.
sections.confidence and gaps:
- **Answer 1 — high confidence.** Every flagged value is directly visible in `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars` and `infra/prod/main.tf`, and `git diff 28f07ee 2f6572d` proves they are unmodified staging copies. The shadow-sink risk rests on `backend/config.py:18-22`, which I read directly. The one judgment call is `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN` — sharing a secret between staging and prod is suspect, but I can't see the secret's contents or intended scope, so "needs a decision" rather than "definitely wrong."
- **Answer 2 — high confidence** on the mechanism and the staging-URL-in-prod defect. Caveat: `frontend/src.txt` is a placeholder describing the app rather than real React source, so I'm trusting its statement (consistent with `vite.config.ts`'s comment and standard Vite behavior) that the app reads `import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL`.
- **Answer 3 — high confidence** that `scripts/deploy.sh` and a `Dockerfile` are absent (exhaustive `find` over the repo) and that the deploy jobs lack checkout. The migration gap is a documented-claim-vs-missing-mechanism finding; I cannot rule out that migrations were intended to run inside the missing `deploy.sh`.
- **Answer 4 — high confidence** on the trust-condition mismatch and the role-name collision, both grounded in the module source, both environments' `main.tf`, and the staging tfstate. Gaps: this repo uses synthetic resources (`synthetic_iam_role`, `token.actions.example.com`), so I'm reasoning from the declared semantics rather than a real cloud provider; and I cannot inspect the GitHub-side `staging`/`production` environment settings (approval rules, secrets) from the repo alone. I also could not run `terraform validate/plan` — no state mutation was permitted and no providers are initialized — so the name-collision claim is inferred from the hardcoded name plus existing state rather than an observed plan error.
sections.next steps:
If I could make changes, in order:

1. Fix `infra/prod/app.auto.tfvars`: `ENABLE_DEBUG_TOOLBAR="false"`, `AUTH_RELAXED="false"`, `PRESET_ADMIN_ACCOUNTS=""`, `SHADOW_SCORING_SINK=""`, and get a decision from the billing owner on a production `LEGACY_BILLING_SECRET_ARN`.
2. Parameterize the role name in `infra/modules/deploy_role/main.tf` (e.g. `medislot-deploy-${var.env}`), set `oidc_sub_condition = "repo:acme-health/medislot:environment:production"` in `infra/prod/main.tf`, and re-apply staging with an explicit name to avoid drift.
3. Add `frontend/.env.production` with the real production API URL and split the pipeline into per-environment builds (or move the API base URL to runtime config), since one `--mode staging` image cannot serve both.
4. Write and commit `scripts/deploy.sh` and a `Dockerfile`; add `actions/checkout` plus an OIDC credentials step (`id-token: write`) to both deploy jobs; add an explicit, visible migration step.
5. Plan the first prod release order: apply `infra/prod`, provision/verify the prod database and run migrations 0001–0003, then run the pipeline; update `docs/prod-runbook.md`, which is three months stale and predates all of this.
6. Get all of the above reviewed and only then `terraform apply infra/prod`.
sections.plan: I set out to review `medislot/infra/prod/` before its first `terraform apply`, checking four things: (1) every production config value against what a real production launch requires, (2) how the frontend bundle gets its API base URL and whether the production deploy ships the right one, (3) each step of the deploy pipeline and whether anything it references actually exists, and (4) the CI-to-cloud authentication path for staging versus production. My method was to read every file in the repo (it is small), diff prod against staging, and check the committed staging Terraform state to see what already exists.
